```text
1	# CLAUDE.md
2	
3	This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
4	
5	## What this is
6	
7	A simplified PTT-style BBS (批踢踢 clone) served over SSH. Built with
8	`charmbracelet/wish` (SSH framework) + `bubbletea` (TUI), persisted in
9	SQLite via the pure-Go `modernc.org/sqlite` driver. **Vim-style navigation
10	is a deliberate product decision** — `h`/`j`/`k`/`l` work alongside arrow
11	keys and Esc/Enter on every list/menu screen. Form screens (register,
12	post compose, water-balloon compose) intentionally do *not* bind h/l so
13	they remain usable as text-edit keys.
14	
15	For the why-we-do-it-this-way notes mapping back to pttbbs internals, see
16	`docs/ptt_trace_code/`. For the testing strategy, see `docs/testing.md`.
17	
18	## Common commands
19	
20	```bash
21	make hostkey            # generate .ssh/host_ed25519 (run once before first run)
22	make run                # boots :2222, creates data/bbs.db with seed boards
23	make build              # produces ./sshbbs
24	make test               # plain go test
25	make test-race          # what we treat as the CI standard
26	make cover              # per-package coverage
27	make db-reset           # rm data/bbs.db*
28	
29	# focused testing
30	go test -run TestPushes_ConcurrentScoreAtomicity -race -v ./internal/store/...
31	go test -race ./internal/chat/...
32	
33	# manual two-user smoke
34	ssh new@localhost -p 2222     # password ignored — fills the in-TUI register form
35	ssh alice@localhost -p 2222   # use the password set during register
36	```
37	
38	If `make hostkey` is re-run, clients see `Host key verification failed`.
39	Fix: `ssh-keygen -R '[localhost]:2222'`.
40	
41	## Architecture — the non-obvious parts
42	
43	### SSH-user-as-login, with `new` as the register sentinel
44	`auth.ReservedUsernameNew = "new"`. The password-auth callback in
45	`internal/server/auth_middleware.go` accepts any password for SSH user
46	`new` and stashes a "register mode" flag in `ssh.Context`; the TUI
47	handler reads it and routes straight to the register screen. For any
48	other SSH username, `auth.VerifyLogin` runs bcrypt against the DB and
49	stashes the `user_id` (int64) in the context for `makeProgramHandler`
50	to load.
51	
52	This means `auth.Register` rejects the literal username `new` — that
53	constant is referenced in two places (auth + middleware) and they must
54	not drift. `auth.go` defines it once.
55	
56	### Wish uses `MiddlewareWithProgramHandler`, not `Middleware`
57	We pick the *ProgramHandler* variant deliberately
58	(`internal/server/server.go`) because we need the `*tea.Program` pointer
59	to register the session with `chat.Broker` and to `program.Send(tea.Quit())`
60	during graceful shutdown. The simpler `Middleware(handler)` form constructs
61	the program internally and hides the pointer — you can't broadcast
62	into it. Don't switch back.
63	
64	### The chat broker takes a `Sender` interface, not `*tea.Program`
65	`chat.Sender` is `interface { Send(tea.Msg) }`. `*tea.Program` satisfies
66	it naturally. The interface exists purely so unit tests can substitute
67	a recording fake (`fakeSender` in `broker_test.go`) without spinning up
68	a real bubbletea program. If you find yourself wanting to mock the
69	broker, you probably want to add a method here, not refactor.
70	
71	### Live broadcasts are "send to everyone, filter on receive"
72	For 推文 (push) updates, `screen_article_view.updatePushInput` calls
73	`broker.SendToAll(senderUID, PushAddedMsg{ArticleID, ...})`. Every
74	connected session receives the message; each article-view model
75	ignores it unless `msg.ArticleID == m.article.ID`. This is simpler than
76	tracking who's viewing what and good enough for our scale. When the
77	filter matches, we **re-fetch from DB** rather than trust the broker
78	payload — that way timestamps and `recommend_score` are always canonical.
79	
80	Water balloons (`broker.Send(toUID, ...)`) target a single user; the
81	multi-session "雙開" case fans out to every live `Session` of that user.
82	
83	### `Store.writeMu` is process-level, not row-level
84	`internal/store/store.go` exposes a single `sync.Mutex` that's grabbed
85	inside every multi-statement repo method (e.g. `PushRepo.Create` runs
86	`INSERT INTO pushes` and `UPDATE articles SET recommend_score` in one
87	transaction under the mutex). This keeps the cached score atomic from
88	the application's perspective and avoids `SQLITE_BUSY` flapping with
89	the pure-Go driver. Don't use `sql.Tx` ad-hoc inside repo methods
90	without also taking `writeMu`. The `pushes` test
91	(`TestPushes_ConcurrentScoreAtomicity`, 50 concurrent inserts) is the
92	canary — if it ever fails under `-race`, the mutex discipline broke.
93	
94	### Migrations live under the store package, not the repo root
95	`internal/store/migrations/*.sql` is embedded with `go:embed
96	migrations/*.sql` from `internal/store/migrate.go`. `go:embed` can't
97	ascend with `..`, so the SQL files live next to the Go code that loads
98	them. Filename format `NNNN_name.sql`; `migrate.apply()` parses the
99	prefix as the version and tracks applied versions in the
100	`schema_migrations` table.
101	
102	### Data deliberately diverges from pttbbs in three places
103	1. **No recno-based article addressing** — `articles.id` (auto-increment)
104	   is opaque and never re-used. Bookmarks stay valid forever.
105	2. **bcrypt cost-12, not `crypt(3)` DES** — `auth.bcryptCost`.
106	3. **No binary shared memory / `userinfo_t` arrays** — presence is a
107	   `sync.RWMutex`-guarded `map[int64][]*Session` in `chat.Broker`.
108	
109	The full mapping (userec_t / boardheader_t / fileheader_t / `.DIR` /
110	推文 / 水球 / userinfo_t) is in `docs/ptt_trace_code/01..06_*.md`.
111	
112	## Testing layers (see `docs/testing.md` for the full doc)
113	
114	- **Layer 1**: plain `go test` for `auth` / `store` / `chat`. Most coverage lives here.
115	- **Layer 2**: direct `tea.Model.Update()` tests in `internal/tui/`. Use
116	  `package tui` (internal), not `tui_test`, to reach unexported screen
117	  constructors. Keybinding-parity tests are table-driven over
118	  `[]string{"esc","backspace","left","h"}` etc. — when adding new keys,
119	  extend the table.
120	- **No `teatest` dependency.** It's experimental (`x/exp`); we test
121	  `Update()` directly with `tea.KeyMsg{...}` synthetic input.
122	- **Always run `-race`.** Several tests (`TestPushes_ConcurrentScoreAtomicity`,
123	  `TestBroker_Concurrency`) exist specifically as race-detector bait.
124	- **Any test needing a DB** uses `storetest.New(t)` which gives a
125	  fresh `t.TempDir()`-backed SQLite. Never share stores across tests.
126	
127	## Conventions worth preserving
128	
129	- **UTF-8 throughout, no Big5.** Use `internal/tui/runewidth.go` helpers
130	  (`PadRight`, `PadLeft`, `Truncate`, `Width`) for any list rendering
131	  that mixes ASCII and CJK — `lipgloss`'s automatic width is unreliable
132	  for double-width glyphs.
133	- **Form screens skip h/l navigation** so the keys remain available for
134	  text editing. Currently: `screen_register`, `screen_post_compose`,
135	  `screen_wb` (compose half).
136	- **NavigateMsg is the only legitimate way to swap screens.** Sub-models
137	  emit it from `Update`; `Root.navigate` is the single switch statement
138	  that constructs the new sub. When you add a new screen, add the case
139	  there and a `Screen*` constant in `messages.go`.
140	- **Window-resize forwarding**: `Root.Update` always forwards
141	  `tea.WindowSizeMsg` to the active sub before its own logic. When
142	  switching to a new sub, `Root.navigate` resends the last known size
143	  so the freshly-mounted screen lays out correctly on first render.
144	
145	<!-- project-knowledge-harness:agent-guidance -->
146	<!-- Snippet for the project's agent contract file (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md /
147	     similar). The bundled scripts/init.sh appends this between sentinel
148	     markers; safe to re-run. -->
149	
150	### Long-term backlog → `TODO.md` + `backlog/`
151	
152	When the user surfaces an idea explicitly **not** being implemented this
153	session (signals: "maybe later", "nice to have", "if I'm interested",
154	"工程量太大需要再評估", "先記下來"), add an entry to [`TODO.md`](TODO.md) using
155	the priority + effort tag schema. Do **not** create new `ROADMAP.md` /
156	`IDEAS.md` / `BACKLOG.md` files — `TODO.md` is the single index.
157	
158	The bundled `scripts/todo-kanban.sh` validates the format. Run it
159	(`scripts/todo-kanban.sh --validate-only TODO.md`) after editing so syntax
160	drift is caught immediately.
161	
162	#### Three ways to add a TODO entry (preferred order)
163	
164	1. **Structured CLI — `scripts/add-todo.sh`** (default):
165	
166	   ```
167	   scripts/add-todo.sh --priority P3 --effort M \
168	     --title "Title" --description "Description"
169	   ```
170	
171	   Inserts a canonically-formatted line into the right `## P*` lane and
172	   re-runs the validator. Add `--backlog` to also scaffold
173	   `backlog/<slug>.md` from the bundled template.
174	
175	2. **Quick capture — `backlog/inbox.md`** (when priority/effort unclear):
176	
177	   ```
178	   echo "- maybe add docs versioning with mike" >> backlog/inbox.md
179	   ```
180	
181	   When the user asks "sweep the inbox", run
182	   `scripts/sweep-inbox.sh`. It prompts for the missing fields per loose
183	   line and calls `add-todo.sh`. Use `--batch` for non-interactive runs
184	   that only formalize lines with parseable `key=value` pairs.
185	
186	3. **Direct edit of `TODO.md`** — fine if the format is fresh; run
187	   `scripts/todo-kanban.sh --validate-only` afterwards.
188	
189	Add a `backlog/<slug>.md` companion doc when the item meets any of:
190	
191	- carries a `P?` tag (record what was tried so it doesn't need re-investigation)
192	- captures a paused troubleshooting session that you intend to fix later
193	  (preserve the error trace + root cause analysis before context evaporates)
194	- weighs multiple options (record trade-offs, not only the winner)
195	- is `[L]` or `[XL]` (architectural; needs design before code)
196	
197	`[S]` items rarely need a backlog doc — a file path in the `TODO.md` line is
198	usually enough. See [`backlog/README.md`](backlog/README.md) for the full
199	template and "when to add a doc" rules.
200	
201	When implementing a `TODO.md` item, in the same commit:
202	
203	1. Run `scripts/promote-todo.sh --title "<substring>" --summary "<what shipped>"`
204	   to move the entry into `## Done` with the dated syntax and re-validate.
205	2. Mark the corresponding `backlog/<slug>.md` (if any) `Status: shipped`
206	   and keep it as a historical record (don't delete — future-you may
207	   revisit adjacent decisions).
208	
209	`backlog/` is excluded from N/A (no packaging — these files stay in the repo) (see N/A); it
210	is repo metadata for maintainers, not user-facing config to deploy.
211	
212	### Past pitfalls → `pitfalls/`
213	
214	When you spend more than ~15 minutes debugging something that wasn't
215	googleable and the fix is non-obvious, write a `pitfalls/<slug>.md`
216	capturing:
217	
218	1. **Verbatim symptom** — copy-paste error messages exactly, do not
219	   paraphrase (preserves grep-ability for future-you / future agent)
220	2. **Root cause** — why this happens (with source / docs / upstream issue link)
221	3. **Workaround** — copy-pasteable commands or config diff
222	4. **Prevention** — how to avoid stepping on this again
223	
224	Title the doc by the **symptom**, not the root cause (you'll search by what
225	you're seeing, not by what you eventually learned). See
226	[`pitfalls/README.md`](pitfalls/README.md) for the full template and
227	when-to-add rules.
228	
229	**Pitfall vs Hard invariant**: a pitfall *graduates* to a Hard invariant in
230	this file when it (a) recurs across machines/agents/sessions despite being
231	documented, (b) silently corrupts state, or (c) the workaround is non-obvious
232	enough that "remember to do X" isn't safe. When graduating, leave the
233	`pitfalls/<slug>.md` as historical record and link to it from the new
234	invariant.
235	
236	`pitfalls/` is excluded from N/A (no packaging — these files stay in the repo) (see N/A) and
237	**not** auto-redacted; review for secrets before committing.
238	<!-- project-knowledge-harness:agent-guidance --> (end)

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./internal/store/migrate.go`

```text
1	package store
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"database/sql"
6		"embed"
7		"fmt"
8		"sort"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11	)
12	
13	//go:embed migrations/*.sql
14	var migrationsFS embed.FS
15	
16	// apply runs every migration whose version number isn't already in
17	// schema_migrations. Filenames must be NNNN_name.sql; the leading integer is
18	// the version. Migrations are applied in ascending version order, each in
19	// its own transaction.
20	func apply(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB) error {
21		if _, err := db.ExecContext(ctx, `
22			CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (
23				version    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
24				applied_at DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
25			)
26		`); err != nil {
27			return fmt.Errorf("create schema_migrations: %w", err)
28		}
29	
30		applied, err := loadApplied(ctx, db)
31		if err != nil {
32			return err
33		}
34	
35		entries, err := migrationsFS.ReadDir("migrations")
36		if err != nil {
37			return fmt.Errorf("read embedded migrations: %w", err)
38		}
39		type migration struct {
40			version  int
41			filename string
42		}
43		var pending []migration
44		for _, e := range entries {
45			if e.IsDir() || !strings.HasSuffix(e.Name(), ".sql") {
46				continue
47			}
48			v, err := parseVersion(e.Name())
49			if err != nil {
50				return err
51			}
52			if !applied[v] {
53				pending = append(pending, migration{version: v, filename: e.Name()})
54			}
55		}
56		sort.Slice(pending, func(i, j int) bool { return pending[i].version < pending[j].version })
57	
58		for _, m := range pending {
59			body, err := migrationsFS.ReadFile("migrations/" + m.filename)
60			if err != nil {
61				return fmt.Errorf("read migration %s: %w", m.filename, err)
62			}
63			if err := runOne(ctx, db, m.version, string(body)); err != nil {
64				return fmt.Errorf("apply %s: %w", m.filename, err)
65			}
66		}
67		return nil
68	}
69	
70	func loadApplied(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB) (map[int]bool, error) {
71		rows, err := db.QueryContext(ctx, `SELECT version FROM schema_migrations`)
72		if err != nil {
73			return nil, fmt.Errorf("load applied migrations: %w", err)
74		}
75		defer rows.Close()
76		out := map[int]bool{}
77		for rows.Next() {
78			var v int
79			if err := rows.Scan(&v); err != nil {
80				return nil, err
81			}
82			out[v] = true
83		}
84		return out, rows.Err()
85	}
86	
87	func runOne(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB, version int, body string) error {
88		tx, err := db.BeginTx(ctx, nil)
89		if err != nil {
90			return err
91		}
92		defer tx.Rollback()
93		if _, err := tx.ExecContext(ctx, body); err != nil {
94			return err
95		}
96		if _, err := tx.ExecContext(ctx, `INSERT INTO schema_migrations(version) VALUES (?)`, version); err != nil {
97			return err
98		}
99		return tx.Commit()
100	}
101	
102	func parseVersion(filename string) (int, error) {
103		// "0001_init.sql" -> 1
104		idx := strings.IndexByte(filename, '_')
105		if idx <= 0 {
106			return 0, fmt.Errorf("migration filename missing version prefix: %s", filename)
107		}
108		v, err := strconv.Atoi(filename[:idx])
109		if err != nil {
110			return 0, fmt.Errorf("parse version in %s: %w", filename, err)
111		}
112		return v, nil
113	}
114
```

# `docs/operations/` — design plan

## 1. Folder name: `operations`

Pick **`operations`**. `devops` is a culture/discipline, not a doc topic — projects that name folders `devops/` end up with one CI YAML and a Dockerfile inside, which is a category error. `lifecycle` is too narrow (sounds like release-only). `ops` is fine but cryptic. `operations` parallels the existing `docs/ptt_trace_code/` (a noun phrase describing the *subject* of the docs), reads naturally in cross-references (`see docs/operations/02_migrations.md`), and admits future siblings like `docs/architecture/` without re-naming.

## 2. File breakdown (5 files, 30-80 lines each)

- **`00_overview.md`** — Index + the milestone ladder (M0-M4) as a single table mapping trigger → mandatory practices. Cross-links to the four deep-dive files. This is the file a future maintainer reads first to locate "where am I, what do I need next?"

- **`01_environments.md`** — dev / stage / prod separation. What changes per env (DB path, hostkey, listen addr, bcrypt cost, log level, seed-data behaviour), why config-as-CLI-flags hits its limit at M2, the migration path to env-vars then a config file. References `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26` and `internal/auth/auth.go:20`.

- **`02_migrations.md`** — Forward-only-now → reversible-later progression. Documents the current `internal/store/migrate.go:20` mechanism, the canonical **expand / migrate / contract** pattern with a worked example (e.g. renaming `articles.recommend_score` → `articles.score_cached`), and a comparison table of `golang-migrate` / `pressly/goose` / `ariga/atlas`. Recommendation: **goose** when reversibility becomes mandatory at M2 (Go-native, supports embedded `fs.FS` like our current setup, in-Go `func up/down` migrations let us reuse `Store.writeMu` for data-rewrite migrations).

- **`03_deployment.md`** — Zero-downtime options ranked by effort. Section 1 covers the current "stop-the-world" graceful shutdown (`cmd/sshbbs/main.go:68-103`) and quantifies its real impact (sub-second for empty broker, capped at 3s drain). Section 2 documents why "true" zero-downtime is *architecturally blocked* by SQLite + in-process broker (the gotcha in §7 below), and lists the three escape hatches in increasing cost: (a) socket-handoff via `SO_REUSEPORT` keeping a single writer (M3), (b) external broker via Redis pub/sub but still single SQLite writer (M3), (c) Postgres + horizontally-scaled stateless servers fronted by an SSH-aware LB (M4). Recommends staying at the M2 "30-second maintenance window" tier indefinitely unless §7 forces escalation.

- **`04_docker.md`** — Multi-stage Dockerfile + `docker-compose.yml` outline. Argues *yes, ship now* (see §5) because the marginal cost is one weekend and the payoff is reproducible local dev + a clean artifact for the eventual CI/CD step. Sketches the stages, volume mounts, and the `compose.yml` shape.

(Five files, not six — keeps it lean and matches `ptt_trace_code/`'s 7-file budget. Backward-compat lives inside `02_migrations.md` rather than getting its own file because the *only* useful answer is "expand/contract pattern", which is wholly a migrations topic.)

## 3. Milestone ladder (M0 → M4)

| Lvl | Trigger to enter | What becomes mandatory |
|---|---|---|
| **M0 — Solo prototype** | now | Forward-only migrations, single CLI-flag config, `db-reset` is fine, no Docker required |
| **M1 — Closed beta** | first non-author user has an account you don't want to lose | Daily SQLite backup (cron + `.backup` command), separate `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db`, host key in version-controlled vault (or 1Password), Dockerfile **+ docker-compose** for reproducible local |
| **M2 — Public beta** | second engineer makes a commit, OR 10 concurrent SSH users | **Reversible migrations** (goose), env-var config, real CI (`go test -race` + `go vet` + `staticcheck` on PR), tagged releases, structured logging via `slog` (already in `TODO.md` P2) |
| **M3 — Production** | first real outage costs you reputation, OR uptime SLA promised to anyone | Stage env that mirrors prod, blue/green-style deploy via socket-handoff *or* SSH-proxy fronting, automated restore drill quarterly, Prometheus metrics |
| **M4 — Scale-out** | single-box CPU or write-throughput hits 70% sustained | Postgres migration, external broker (Redis), horizontal SSH frontends, stateless app tier — **only** if M3 is no longer enough |

The single trigger format means you don't argue about whether to bump — you just check the trigger.

## 4. Migration tooling recommendation

**Stay on the homegrown `internal/store/migrate.go` through M1.** It's 113 lines, embedded, transactional, tested by being used. Replacing it now is wasted motion.

**At M2 switch to `pressly/goose`.** Specifically because:
- Native Go (`func upXxx(tx *sql.Tx)`) migrations let data rewrites participate in `Store.writeMu`, which the SQL-only competitors can't.
- Has `fs.FS` support — drop-in replacement for our current `go:embed migrations/*.sql`.
- Down migrations are first-class but optional per file; doesn't force them on M0/M1-style additions.
- Smaller dep footprint than `atlas` (which brings a schema-diff engine we don't need) and more Go-idiomatic than `golang-migrate` (which is heavier and treats Go migrations as a second-class extension).

`atlas` is the right pick *only* if you commit to declarative schemas (write the desired state, atlas computes the diff). That's a different working style and overkill for a 6-table BBS.

The **expand / migrate / contract** pattern, mandatory from M2 onward:
1. **Expand** — additive migration: add new column nullable, add new table, dual-write from app code.
2. **Migrate** — backfill data, deploy a release that reads from new + writes to both.
3. **Contract** — once all instances are on N, drop the old column/table in a third migration.

The point is each individual deploy is backward-compatible with the previous binary. M0/M1 can ignore this; M2+ cannot.

## 5. Dockerfile + compose: ship now

**Yes, do it now.** Reasoning: the project already has zero `cgo` dependencies (`modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go), which is the rarest and hardest-won precondition for a *trivial* multi-stage build. Skipping it means re-discovering that fact later.

Outline (sketch only — `04_docker.md` carries the actual text):

```dockerfile
# --- builder ---
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -trimpath -ldflags="-s -w" -o /out/sshbbs ./cmd/sshbbs

# --- final ---
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /out/sshbbs /sshbbs
USER nonroot
EXPOSE 2222
ENTRYPOINT ["/sshbbs", "-addr=:2222", "-db=/data/bbs.db", "-hostkey=/keys/host_ed25519"]
```

`docker-compose.yml` mounts two named volumes (`bbs-data` for `/data`, `bbs-keys` for `/keys`), maps `2222:2222`, and includes a one-shot init service that runs `gen-hostkey.sh` if `/keys/host_ed25519` is absent. `make compose-up` becomes the new "first run" command for contributors — replaces the README's current 3-step setup.

Distroless over scratch because it provides `/etc/passwd` for the `nonroot` user and basic CA certs for free, at ~2MB cost.

## 6. Candidate `TODO.md` entries

Six entries fit the existing `[Priority/Effort]` schema:

- `[P3/M] Multi-stage Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml` — produces reproducible local dev; backlog doc unnecessary, points at `docs/operations/04_docker.md`.
- `[P3/M] Migrate config from CLI flags to env vars (M2 prep)` — additive, env vars override flags. Touches `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22`.
- `[P3/M] Switch migration runner to pressly/goose` — gated on M2 entry (second engineer or reversibility need). Replaces `internal/store/migrate.go`. Backlog doc warranted ([L]-flavoured even if [M] code-wise — design decision).
- `[P?/L] External chat broker (Redis pub/sub) for zero-downtime` — needs spike on whether socket-handoff is cheaper. Backlog doc mandatory (P? + L).
- `[P?/XL] Postgres migration plan` — only if M4 ever triggers. Spike doc captures the SQLite-WAL → Postgres delta. P? + XL → backlog mandatory.
- `[P3/S] CI workflow: test-race + vet + staticcheck on PR` — small, gates M2. GitHub Actions YAML, ~40 lines.

## 7. Risks / gotchas — call these out in `00_overview.md`

You named the big one. Add four total:

1. **SQLite WAL forbids two writers on the same file.** Blue/green deploy with the current architecture means *both* binaries open `data/bbs.db` simultaneously — second writer either gets `SQLITE_BUSY` storms or, worse, corruption if filesystem-level locks aren't honoured (NFS, some Docker volume drivers). Either single-writer architecture forever or Postgres at M4. Document in `03_deployment.md` and `04_docker.md` (don't put DB volume on NFS).

2. **In-memory broker means session-loss on every restart.** No amount of graceful shutdown saves the live presence map (`chat.Broker.sessions`). Persistent water balloons & mail survive (see `internal/store/migrate.go:0003_add_mail.sql`), but online-list flickers. Documented behaviour, not a bug — but a hard ceiling on uptime perception until M3.

3. **bcrypt cost-12 is a CPU spike on cold start.** If M3 ever puts you behind a load balancer with active health checks, repeated logins from monitoring will burn cores. Note in `01_environments.md`: prod env can lower to cost-10 if needed, but every existing hash stays valid (bcrypt embeds the cost).

4. **`go:embed migrations/*.sql` is a one-way street out of the binary.** A goose switch must keep the embed approach (`fs.FS` interface) — running migrations from disk in a container means every deploy needs the SQL files mounted, which is a regression.

The first one is the architectural ceiling. Everything above M2 in the ladder is shaped by it.

```text
1	# 00 · Overview — what we mimic from pttbbs and what we drop
2	
3	These notes trace pttbbs (https://github.com/ptt/pttbbs) concepts to what we
4	actually implement in this repo. They exist so future readers know which design
5	decisions are intentional simplifications vs. things still to do.
6	
7	## What we mimic
8	
9	| pttbbs concept                | Where in our repo                                 |
10	|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
11	| `userec_t` (user record)      | `users` table + `internal/store/users.go`         |
12	| `boardheader_t` (board)       | `boards` table + `internal/store/boards.go`       |
13	| `fileheader_t` (article hdr)  | `articles` table + `internal/store/articles.go`   |
14	| `.DIR` per-board index        | `articles.board_id` + `idx_articles_board_created`|
15	| 推文 (push counter / log)      | `pushes` table + `articles.recommend_score` cache |
16	| 水球 (water balloon, live IPC) | `internal/chat/broker.go` (in-memory)             |
17	| `userinfo_t` UTMP table       | `Broker.sessions map[int64][]*Session` (in-mem)   |
18	| `mbbsd` per-session process   | one bubbletea program per SSH session             |
19	
20	## What we drop on purpose (v1)
21	
22	- **Binary on-disk shared memory** (`SHM`, `userinfo_t` arrays). pttbbs uses
23	  fixed-size mmap'd structs; we use SQLite + Go maps. Adds 5-10× the RAM but
24	  removes ~80% of pttbbs's complexity (alignment bugs, sizeof drift, daemon
25	  coordination).
26	- **`recno`-based article addressing.** `.DIR` indexes articles by record
27	  number, which leaves tombstones on delete and breaks bookmarks if you renumber.
28	  We use `articles.id` (auto-increment) and never reuse IDs.
29	- **Big5 encoding.** UTF-8 throughout. `go-runewidth` handles double-width
30	  CJK. No transcoding layer.
31	- **DES `crypt(3)` passwords.** bcrypt (cost 12) instead.
32	- **The `mbbsd` / `logind` / `boardd` / `commentd` / `utmpd` daemon split.**
33	  One Go process owns everything. PTT split because forking C processes
34	  per-session was the only way to scale on 1990s hardware; goroutines remove
35	  the constraint.
36	- **Games, BBSMovie, voting, mail-to-external-email, post-tracking AID system,
37	  archive/digest.** All deferred to P2 or out of scope.
38	
39	## What we kept simple but recognisable
40	
41	- **PTT-style login: SSH user is the login name.** `ssh alice@host` lands
42	  alice on her account (after SSH password auth verifies against bcrypt).
43	  `ssh new@host` is the in-TUI register sentinel.
44	- **Boards as flat list.** No hierarchy / categories yet (P1).
45	- **推 / 噓 / →** with single-key input (`+` / `-` / `=`) and live broadcast
46	  to other viewers of the same article.
47	- **One-line water balloons** with offline persistence and on-reconnect replay.
48	
49	## Files in this directory
50	
51	- `00_overview.md` — this file
52	- `01_userec.md` — user record mapping
53	- `02_boardheader.md` — board record mapping
54	- `03_fileheader_dir.md` — article + DIR mapping
55	- `04_push_comment.md` — 推文 design
56	- `05_water_balloon.md` — 水球 design
57	- `06_session_userinfo.md` — online presence design
58
```
</details></tool-use>

24		dbPath := flag.String("db", "data/bbs.db", "SQLite database path")
25		hostkey := flag.String("hostkey", ".ssh/host_ed25519", "SSH host key path")
26		flag.Parse()
27	
28		st, err := store.Open(*dbPath)
29		if err != nil {
30			log.Fatalf("store.Open: %v", err)
31		}
32		defer st.Close()
33		if err := st.Boards().SeedDefaults(context.Background()); err != nil {
34			log.Fatalf("seed boards: %v", err)
35		}
36		log.Printf("storage ready at %s", *dbPath)
37	
38		broker := chat.NewBroker()
39	
40		srv, err := server.New(server.Config{Addr: *addr, HostKey: *hostkey}, st, broker)
41		if err != nil {
42			log.Fatalf("server.New: %v", err)
43		}
44	
45		ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)
46		defer stop()
47	
48		errCh := make(chan error, 1)
49		go func() {
50			log.Printf("sshbbs %s listening on %s", version.Version, *addr)
51			errCh <- srv.ListenAndServe()
52		}()
53	
54		select {
55		case err := <-errCh:
56			if err != nil && !errors.Is(err, ssh.ErrServerClosed) {
57				log.Fatalf("listen: %v", err)
58			}
59		case <-ctx.Done():
60			log.Println("shutdown signal received")
61			shutdown(srv, broker)
62		}
63	}
64	
65	// shutdown closes the listener (refuses new connections), then asks every
66	// live bubbletea program to quit, then closes the SSH server hard. The DB
67	// closes on the deferred st.Close() in main.
68	func shutdown(srv *ssh.Server, broker *chat.Broker) {
69		const drainTimeout = 3 * time.Second
70	
71		// 1. Stop accepting new connections.
72		listenerCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), drainTimeout)
73		defer cancel()
74		if err := srv.Shutdown(listenerCtx); err != nil {
75			log.Printf("server.Shutdown: %v", err)
76		}
77	
78		// 2. Tell every active session to quit.
79		sessions := broker.SessionsSnapshot()
80		if len(sessions) == 0 {
81			return
82		}
83		log.Printf("draining %d active session(s)", len(sessions))
84		for _, s := range sessions {
85			s.Program.Send(tea.Quit())
86		}
87	
88		// 3. Give them a moment to flush their final view, then force-close.
89		timer := time.NewTimer(drainTimeout)
90		defer timer.Stop()
91		for {
92			if len(broker.SessionsSnapshot()) == 0 {
93				log.Println("all sessions drained")
94				return
95			}
96			select {
97			case <-timer.C:
98				log.Printf("drain timeout; %d session(s) still attached", len(broker.SessionsSnapshot()))
99				return
100			case <-time.After(100 * time.Millisecond):
101			}
102		}
103	}
104
```

**What should the new docs subfolder be named?**

Options:
- **docs/operations/** - Mirrors the noun-phrase pattern of docs/ptt_trace_code/ — describes the subject. Recommended.
- **docs/devops/** - More discoverable but conflates topic with discipline; usually ends up overloaded.
- **docs/lifecycle/** - Emphasizes the milestone-ladder framing but reads release-only, narrower than scope.
- **docs/ops/** - Short and conventional, but cryptic for new readers.

**Should we ship a Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml alongside the docs, or only document the recommended shape?**

Options:
- **Ship both Dockerfile + compose now** - Multi-stage Dockerfile (distroless) + docker-compose.yml that auto-generates host key on first run. Pure-Go SQLite makes this cheap. Recommended.
- **Doc-only with example snippets** - Embed the multi-stage Dockerfile and compose YAML inside docs/operations/04_docker.md as recommendations; do not commit working files yet.
- **Compose only (no Dockerfile)** - Compose runs `go build` on the host and binds the binary in. Simpler, but loses the artifact-portability benefit.

**Should the docs be in English (matching docs/ptt_trace_code/) or Traditional Chinese?**

Options:
- **English** - Matches existing docs/ptt_trace_code/ + docs/testing.md exactly. Recommended for consistency.
- **English with zh-TW summary blocks** - Body in English, but each file gets a short zh-TW TL;DR block at the top so the milestone framing reads naturally for you.
- **Traditional Chinese** - Optimized for the user; diverges from the surrounding doc set.

**Should we also add 4-6 milestone-gated TODO.md entries (e.g. P3/M Dockerfile, P?/L external broker, P3/S CI workflow) so the doc cross-links to actionable work?**

Options:
- **Yes — add TODO.md entries + needed backlog/ docs** - Use scripts/add-todo.sh per CLAUDE.md convention. P? + L/XL items get a backlog/<slug>.md companion. Recommended.
- **Yes — TODO.md only, no backlog/ docs** - Faster; loses the design-rationale capture for the [L]/[XL]/P? items.
- **No — docs only, leave TODO.md alone** - Keeps this PR docs-only. Worth doing if you want zero TODO churn until you decide which milestone trigger you're closest to.


**Answer:** docs/operations/, Ship both Dockerfile + compose now, English, Yes — add TODO.md entries + needed backlog/ docs

# Plan: `docs/operations/` + Dockerfile + Milestone-Gated TODO Entries

## Context

The project is in MVP/prototype phase — DB schema is "wild west", config is
CLI-flags-only, no Docker, no CI. The user wants reference documentation for
*when and how* to graduate to more formal practices (reversible migrations,
dev/stage/prod separation, zero-downtime deploys) so future-them or a future
collaborator can recognize the trigger and execute without re-researching.

The deliverable has three parts:

1. **`docs/operations/`** — five English markdown files mirroring the
   `docs/ptt_trace_code/` style (30-80 lines each, numbered, code-cross-referenced).
2. **Dockerfile + `docker-compose.yml`** — a multi-stage build + a one-command
   local-dev compose that handles host-key generation and volume persistence.
   Cheap because `modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go (no cgo).
3. **Milestone-gated TODO.md entries + backlog docs** — six entries surface
   the future-work ladder so it's visible from the index, not just the docs.

Architectural ceiling that shapes everything: **SQLite WAL forbids two
concurrent writers on the same DB file**. Combined with the in-memory
`chat.Broker` (`sessions map[int64][]*Session`), this means *true*
zero-downtime deploys require either (a) a single-writer architecture forever,
or (b) Postgres at the M4 milestone. The docs name this constraint up front.

---

## File layout

New files (all created in this batch):

\```
docs/operations/
  00_overview.md         # index + the M0→M4 milestone ladder + the architectural ceiling
  01_environments.md     # dev/stage/prod separation; CLI flags → env vars → config file
  02_migrations.md       # forward-only → reversible (goose) → expand/migrate/contract
  03_deployment.md       # graceful shutdown today + zero-downtime escape hatches
  04_docker.md           # the shipped Dockerfile + compose.yml explained

Dockerfile                # multi-stage: golang:1.23-alpine → distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
docker-compose.yml        # service + named volumes (bbs-data, bbs-keys) + init service for hostkey
.dockerignore             # exclude data/, .ssh/, .git/, .specstory/, .claude/, etc.

backlog/
  goose-migration-switch.md         # design notes for the M2 goose migration
  external-chat-broker.md           # spike for P?/L Redis pub/sub broker
  postgres-migration-plan.md        # spike for P?/XL Postgres at M4
\```

Files modified:

- `TODO.md` — six new entries via `scripts/add-todo.sh` (validates on insert)
- `Makefile` — three new targets: `compose-up`, `compose-down`, `docker-build`
- `README.md` — add a "Local dev with Docker" subsection pointing at compose

Files **not** modified (the docs-only contract): no Go source, no migrations,
no `cmd/sshbbs/main.go`. The docs describe future changes; the code stays put.

---

## The five docs (content sketch)

### `00_overview.md` — index + milestone ladder

Opens with the same "What this is / what we mimic" framing as
`docs/ptt_trace_code/00_overview.md:1-7`. Two tables:

**Table 1 — milestone ladder.** Single trigger per row (the bump rule):

| Lvl | Trigger to enter | What becomes mandatory |
|---|---|---|
| **M0** Solo prototype | now | Forward-only migrations, CLI-flag config, `make db-reset` is fine, no Docker required |
| **M1** Closed beta | first non-author user has an account you don't want to lose | Daily SQLite backup (cron + `.backup`), separate `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db`, host key in vault, **Dockerfile + compose** for reproducible local |
| **M2** Public beta | second engineer makes a commit, OR 10 concurrent SSH users | Reversible migrations (goose), env-var config, CI on PR (`go test -race` + `vet` + `staticcheck`), tagged releases, `slog` (P2 in `TODO.md`) |
| **M3** Production | first real outage, OR uptime SLA promised | Stage env mirroring prod, blue/green via socket-handoff *or* SSH-proxy, automated restore drill, Prometheus metrics |
| **M4** Scale-out | single-box CPU or write-throughput sustained at 70% | Postgres migration, external broker (Redis), horizontal SSH frontends — only if M3 is no longer enough |

**Table 2 — architectural ceilings to flag now**:

1. SQLite WAL forbids two writers → blue/green needs single-writer architecture or Postgres
2. In-memory `chat.Broker` → restart drops live presence (water balloons & mail are persistent, so survive)
3. bcrypt cost-12 → CPU spike on cold start; M3 health-check storms can burn cores
4. `go:embed migrations/*.sql` → migration runner switch must keep the `fs.FS` interface

### `01_environments.md` — dev / stage / prod separation

What changes per env: DB path, hostkey path, listen addr, bcrypt cost, log
level, seed-data behavior. Why CLI flags hit their limit at M2: secret
management (you can't put a hostkey passphrase on a flag without it landing in
`ps`), per-env override sets become an N×M matrix. The progression:

1. **M0–M1**: CLI flags + `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db` filename split
2. **M2**: env-vars override flags (`SSHBBS_DB`, `SSHBBS_ADDR`, etc.) — additive,
   doesn't break the flag interface. Touches `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26`.
3. **M3**: per-env config file (TOML/YAML) checked into infra repo, secrets
   from a real KMS (not the config). The flag/env interface stays as override.

References `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26` (config surface) and
`internal/auth/auth.go:20` (`bcryptCost = 12` — note in §M3 it can drop to 10
without invalidating existing hashes since bcrypt embeds cost).

### `02_migrations.md` — forward-only → reversible → expand/migrate/contract

Section 1 documents the current mechanism (`internal/store/migrate.go:13-103`):
embedded `migrations/NNNN_name.sql`, `schema_migrations` table tracks version,
each migration runs in `tx.BeginTx`. Forward-only is *correct* for M0/M1.

Section 2 — when reversibility becomes mandatory (M2 entry trigger). Comparison
table:

| Tool | Pros | Cons | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| `golang-migrate/migrate` | most popular | Go migrations are second-class; heavier dep | skip |
| `pressly/goose` | native Go func migrations participate in `Store.writeMu`; `fs.FS` support keeps `go:embed`; smaller dep | smaller community | **recommended at M2** |
| `ariga/atlas` | declarative schema diff | different working style; overkill for 6 tables | skip unless going schema-as-code |

Section 3 — the **expand / migrate / contract** pattern, mandatory from M2:

1. **Expand**: additive migration (add nullable column, new table). Old + new
   binary both work.
2. **Migrate**: backfill data; deploy a release that dual-writes/dual-reads.
3. **Contract**: drop old column/table once all instances are on the new code.

Worked example: renaming `articles.recommend_score` → `articles.score_cached`.
Three migrations, three deploys. Each individual deploy is backward-compatible
with the previous binary.

### `03_deployment.md` — graceful shutdown today + zero-downtime options

Section 1 documents what already works (`cmd/sshbbs/main.go:65-103`): SIGTERM
→ refuse new connections (`srv.Shutdown`) → `program.Send(tea.Quit())` to
every live bubbletea program → poll `broker.SessionsSnapshot()` with 3s
timeout → DB close. Real-world impact is sub-second for an empty broker,
capped at 3s for an active one. **For M0–M2, this is enough — accept a 3s
maintenance blip.**

Section 2 — why "true" zero-downtime is architecturally blocked here:
SQLite-two-writers + in-memory broker. Three escape hatches in increasing
cost (recommend the cheapest that fits the milestone):

- **(a) Socket-handoff via `SO_REUSEPORT`** (M3): old binary keeps existing
  sessions, new binary takes new connections, old binary exits when last
  session closes. Single SQLite writer at a time.
- **(b) External broker (Redis pub/sub)** (M3): broker becomes stateless;
  new binary inherits presence from Redis. Still single SQLite writer.
- **(c) Postgres + horizontal stateless servers fronted by SSH-aware LB** (M4):
  the only path for *true* concurrent writers. Big rewrite.

Recommendation: **stay at M2's 30-second-maintenance-window tier indefinitely
unless §00 architectural ceiling forces escalation.**

### `04_docker.md` — the shipped Dockerfile + compose explained

Walks through the actual files committed in this PR (so the doc and the
artifact stay in sync). Key shape decisions:

- **distroless/static-debian12:nonroot** over scratch: `~2MB` extra for free
  CA certs and `/etc/passwd` (lets `USER nonroot` work). Worth it.
- **`CGO_ENABLED=0`** in builder: pure-Go SQLite makes this trivial; static
  binary works in any final image.
- **Two named volumes**: `bbs-data` for `/data` (the SQLite WAL files),
  `bbs-keys` for `/keys` (host key persists across container restarts).
- **Init service in compose**: runs `gen-hostkey.sh` only if
  `/keys/host_ed25519` is missing — first `docker compose up` Just Works.
- **Volume driver warning**: do **not** put `bbs-data` on NFS or some Docker
  volume drivers — SQLite filesystem locks aren't honoured everywhere, and
  a corrupt WAL is much worse than a 3s maintenance window.

`make compose-up` becomes the new "first run" command for contributors,
replacing the README's current 3-step `make hostkey && make run` setup.

---

## The Dockerfile + compose (final shape, not just sketch)

### `Dockerfile`

\```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
ARG VERSION=dev
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build \
    -trimpath \
    -ldflags="-s -w -X github.com/daviddwlee84/sshbbs/internal/version.Version=${VERSION}" \
    -o /out/sshbbs ./cmd/sshbbs

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /out/sshbbs /sshbbs
USER nonroot
EXPOSE 2222
VOLUME ["/data", "/keys"]
ENTRYPOINT ["/sshbbs", "-addr=:2222", "-db=/data/bbs.db", "-hostkey=/keys/host_ed25519"]
\```

### `docker-compose.yml`

\```yaml
services:
  hostkey-init:
    image: alpine:3.20
    volumes:
      - bbs-keys:/keys
    entrypoint: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    command:
      - |
        if [ ! -f /keys/host_ed25519 ]; then
          apk add --no-cache openssh-keygen >/dev/null
          ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f /keys/host_ed25519 -N "" -q
          chmod 600 /keys/host_ed25519
        fi
  sshbbs:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      hostkey-init:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
    ports:
      - "2222:2222"
    volumes:
      - bbs-data:/data
      - bbs-keys:/keys
    restart: unless-stopped
volumes:
  bbs-data:
  bbs-keys:
\```

### `.dockerignore`

\```
.git/
.github/
.specstory/
.claude/
data/
.ssh/
sshbbs
*.test
docs/demo.gif
docs/demo.webm
backlog/
pitfalls/
\```

### `Makefile` additions

\```makefile
compose-up:
	docker compose up --build -d
compose-down:
	docker compose down
docker-build:
	docker build -t sshbbs:dev .
\```

---

The `--backlog` flag scaffolds `backlog/<slug>.md` from the template. Three

dev/stage/prod separation, zero-downtime deploys) so future-them or a future
collaborator can recognize the trigger and execute without re-researching.

The deliverable has three parts:

1. **`docs/operations/`** — five English markdown files mirroring the
   `docs/ptt_trace_code/` style (30-80 lines each, numbered, code-cross-referenced).
2. **Dockerfile + `docker-compose.yml`** — a multi-stage build + a one-command
   local-dev compose that handles host-key generation and volume persistence.
   Cheap because `modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go (no cgo).

   the future-work ladder so it's visible from the index, not just the docs.

Architectural ceiling that shapes everything: **SQLite WAL forbids two
concurrent writers on the same DB file**. Combined with the in-memory

Files **not** modified (the docs-only contract): no Go source, no migrations,
no `cmd/sshbbs/main.go`. The docs describe future changes; the code stays put.

---

## The five docs (content sketch)

### `00_overview.md` — index + milestone ladder

Opens with the same "What this is / what we mimic" framing as
`docs/ptt_trace_code/00_overview.md:1-7`. Two tables:

**Table 1 — milestone ladder.** Single trigger per row (the bump rule):

| Lvl | Trigger to enter | What becomes mandatory |
|---|---|---|
| **M0** Solo prototype | now | Forward-only migrations, CLI-flag config, `make db-reset` is fine, no Docker required |
| **M1** Closed beta | first non-author user has an account you don't want to lose | Daily SQLite backup (cron + `.backup`), separate `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db`, host key in vault, **Dockerfile + compose** for reproducible local |
| **M2** Public beta | second engineer makes a commit, OR 10 concurrent SSH users | Reversible migrations (goose), env-var config, CI on PR (`go test -race` + `vet` + `staticcheck`), tagged releases, `slog` (P2 in `TODO.md`) |
| **M3** Production | first real outage, OR uptime SLA promised | Stage env mirroring prod, blue/green via socket-handoff *or* SSH-proxy, automated restore drill, Prometheus metrics |
| **M4** Scale-out | single-box CPU or write-throughput sustained at 70% | Postgres migration, external broker (Redis), horizontal SSH frontends — only if M3 is no longer enough |

**Table 2 — architectural ceilings to flag now**:

1. SQLite WAL forbids two writers → blue/green needs single-writer architecture or Postgres
2. In-memory `chat.Broker` → restart drops live presence (water balloons & mail are persistent, so survive)
3. bcrypt cost-12 → CPU spike on cold start; M3 health-check storms can burn cores
4. `go:embed migrations/*.sql` → migration runner switch must keep the `fs.FS` interface

### `01_environments.md` — dev / stage / prod separation

What changes per env: DB path, hostkey path, listen addr, bcrypt cost, log
level, seed-data behavior. Why CLI flags hit their limit at M2: secret
management (you can't put a hostkey passphrase on a flag without it landing in
`ps`), per-env override sets become an N×M matrix. The progression:

1. **M0–M1**: CLI flags + `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db` filename split
2. **M2**: env-vars override flags (`SSHBBS_DB`, `SSHBBS_ADDR`, etc.) — additive,
   doesn't break the flag interface. Touches `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26`.
3. **M3**: per-env config file (TOML/YAML) checked into infra repo, secrets
   from a real KMS (not the config). The flag/env interface stays as override.

References `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26` (config surface) and
`internal/auth/auth.go:20` (`bcryptCost = 12` — note in §M3 it can drop to 10
without invalidating existing hashes since bcrypt embeds cost).

### `02_migrations.md` — forward-only → reversible → expand/migrate/contract

Section 1 documents the current mechanism (`internal/store/migrate.go:13-103`):
embedded `migrations/NNNN_name.sql`, `schema_migrations` table tracks version,
each migration runs in `tx.BeginTx`. Forward-only is *correct* for M0/M1.

Section 2 — when reversibility becomes mandatory (M2 entry trigger). Comparison
table:

| Tool | Pros | Cons | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| `golang-migrate/migrate` | most popular | Go migrations are second-class; heavier dep | skip |
| `pressly/goose` | native Go func migrations participate in `Store.writeMu`; `fs.FS` support keeps `go:embed`; smaller dep | smaller community | **recommended at M2** |
| `ariga/atlas` | declarative schema diff | different working style; overkill for 6 tables | skip unless going schema-as-code |

Section 3 — the **expand / migrate / contract** pattern, mandatory from M2:

1. **Expand**: additive migration (add nullable column, new table). Old + new
   binary both work.
2. **Migrate**: backfill data; deploy a release that dual-writes/dual-reads.
3. **Contract**: drop old column/table once all instances are on the new code.

Worked example: renaming `articles.recommend_score` → `articles.score_cached`.
Three migrations, three deploys. Each individual deploy is backward-compatible
with the previous binary.

### `03_deployment.md` — graceful shutdown today + zero-downtime options

Section 1 documents what already works (`cmd/sshbbs/main.go:65-103`): SIGTERM
→ refuse new connections (`srv.Shutdown`) → `program.Send(tea.Quit())` to
every live bubbletea program → poll `broker.SessionsSnapshot()` with 3s
timeout → DB close. Real-world impact is sub-second for an empty broker,
capped at 3s for an active one. **For M0–M2, this is enough — accept a 3s
maintenance blip.**

Section 2 — why "true" zero-downtime is architecturally blocked here:
SQLite-two-writers + in-memory broker. Three escape hatches in increasing
cost (recommend the cheapest that fits the milestone):

- **(a) Socket-handoff via `SO_REUSEPORT`** (M3): old binary keeps existing
  sessions, new binary takes new connections, old binary exits when last
  session closes. Single SQLite writer at a time.
- **(b) External broker (Redis pub/sub)** (M3): broker becomes stateless;
  new binary inherits presence from Redis. Still single SQLite writer.
- **(c) Postgres + horizontal stateless servers fronted by SSH-aware LB** (M4):
  the only path for *true* concurrent writers. Big rewrite.

Recommendation: **stay at M2's 30-second-maintenance-window tier indefinitely
unless §00 architectural ceiling forces escalation.**

### `04_docker.md` — the shipped Dockerfile + compose explained

Walks through the actual files committed in this PR (so the doc and the
artifact stay in sync). Key shape decisions:

- **distroless/static-debian12:nonroot** over scratch: `~2MB` extra for free
  CA certs and `/etc/passwd` (lets `USER nonroot` work). Worth it.
- **`CGO_ENABLED=0`** in builder: pure-Go SQLite makes this trivial; static
  binary works in any final image.
- **Two named volumes**: `bbs-data` for `/data` (the SQLite WAL files),
  `bbs-keys` for `/keys` (host key persists across container restarts).
- **Init service in compose**: runs `gen-hostkey.sh` only if
  `/keys/host_ed25519` is missing — first `docker compose up` Just Works.
- **Volume driver warning**: do **not** put `bbs-data` on NFS or some Docker
  volume drivers — SQLite filesystem locks aren't honoured everywhere, and
  a corrupt WAL is much worse than a 3s maintenance window.

`make compose-up` becomes the new "first run" command for contributors,
replacing the README's current 3-step `make hostkey && make run` setup.

---

## The Dockerfile + compose (final shape, not just sketch)

### `Dockerfile`

```dockerfile
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
ARG VERSION=dev
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build \
    -trimpath \
    -ldflags="-s -w -X github.com/daviddwlee84/sshbbs/internal/version.Version=${VERSION}" \
    -o /out/sshbbs ./cmd/sshbbs

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /out/sshbbs /sshbbs
USER nonroot
EXPOSE 2222
VOLUME ["/data", "/keys"]
ENTRYPOINT ["/sshbbs", "-addr=:2222", "-db=/data/bbs.db", "-hostkey=/keys/host_ed25519"]
```

### `docker-compose.yml`

```yaml
services:
  hostkey-init:
    image: alpine:3.20
    volumes:
      - bbs-keys:/keys
    entrypoint: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    command:
      - |
        if [ ! -f /keys/host_ed25519 ]; then
          apk add --no-cache openssh-keygen >/dev/null
          ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f /keys/host_ed25519 -N "" -q
          chmod 600 /keys/host_ed25519
        fi
  sshbbs:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      hostkey-init:
        condition: service_completed_successfully
    ports:
      - "2222:2222"
    volumes:
      - bbs-data:/data
      - bbs-keys:/keys
    restart: unless-stopped
volumes:
  bbs-data:
  bbs-keys:
```

### `.dockerignore`

```
.git/
.github/
.specstory/
.claude/
data/
.ssh/
sshbbs
*.test
docs/demo.gif
docs/demo.webm
backlog/
pitfalls/
```

### `Makefile` additions

```makefile
compose-up:
	docker compose up --build -d
compose-down:
	docker compose down
docker-build:
	docker build -t sshbbs:dev .
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./internal/store/migrate.go`

```text
1	package store
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"database/sql"
6		"embed"
7		"fmt"
8		"sort"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11	)
12	
13	//go:embed migrations/*.sql
14	var migrationsFS embed.FS
15	
16	// apply runs every migration whose version number isn't already in
17	// schema_migrations. Filenames must be NNNN_name.sql; the leading integer is
18	// the version. Migrations are applied in ascending version order, each in
19	// its own transaction.
20	func apply(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB) error {
21		if _, err := db.ExecContext(ctx, `
22			CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (
23				version    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
24				applied_at DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
25			)
26		`); err != nil {
27			return fmt.Errorf("create schema_migrations: %w", err)
28		}
29	
30		applied, err := loadApplied(ctx, db)
31		if err != nil {
32			return err
33		}
34	
35		entries, err := migrationsFS.ReadDir("migrations")
36		if err != nil {
37			return fmt.Errorf("read embedded migrations: %w", err)
38		}
39		type migration struct {
40			version  int
41			filename string
42		}
43		var pending []migration
44		for _, e := range entries {
45			if e.IsDir() || !strings.HasSuffix(e.Name(), ".sql") {
46				continue
47			}
48			v, err := parseVersion(e.Name())
49			if err != nil {
50				return err
51			}
52			if !applied[v] {
53				pending = append(pending, migration{version: v, filename: e.Name()})
54			}
55		}
56		sort.Slice(pending, func(i, j int) bool { return pending[i].version < pending[j].version })
57	
58		for _, m := range pending {
59			body, err := migrationsFS.ReadFile("migrations/" + m.filename)
60			if err != nil {
61				return fmt.Errorf("read migration %s: %w", m.filename, err)
62			}
63			if err := runOne(ctx, db, m.version, string(body)); err != nil {
64				return fmt.Errorf("apply %s: %w", m.filename, err)
65			}
66		}
67		return nil
68	}
69	
70	func loadApplied(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB) (map[int]bool, error) {
71		rows, err := db.QueryContext(ctx, `SELECT version FROM schema_migrations`)
72		if err != nil {
73			return nil, fmt.Errorf("load applied migrations: %w", err)
74		}
75		defer rows.Close()
76		out := map[int]bool{}
77		for rows.Next() {
78			var v int
79			if err := rows.Scan(&v); err != nil {
80				return nil, err
81			}
82			out[v] = true
83		}
84		return out, rows.Err()
85	}
86	
87	func runOne(ctx context.Context, db *sql.DB, version int, body string) error {
88		tx, err := db.BeginTx(ctx, nil)
89		if err != nil {
90			return err
91		}
92		defer tx.Rollback()
93		if _, err := tx.ExecContext(ctx, body); err != nil {
94			return err
95		}
96		if _, err := tx.ExecContext(ctx, `INSERT INTO schema_migrations(version) VALUES (?)`, version); err != nil {
97			return err
98		}
99		return tx.Commit()
100	}
101	
102	func parseVersion(filename string) (int, error) {
103		// "0001_init.sql" -> 1
104		idx := strings.IndexByte(filename, '_')
105		if idx <= 0 {
106			return 0, fmt.Errorf("migration filename missing version prefix: %s", filename)
107		}
108		v, err := strconv.Atoi(filename[:idx])
109		if err != nil {
110			return 0, fmt.Errorf("parse version in %s: %w", filename, err)
111		}
112		return v, nil
113	}
114
```

 `./internal/chat/broker.go`

```text
1	// Package chat is the in-memory hub that connects live SSH sessions for
2	// real-time features (water balloons, push broadcasts). It mirrors PTT's
3	// `userinfo_t` shared-memory table conceptually, but as a process-local
4	// map[user_id][]*Session — no SHM, no daemons.
5	package chat
6	
7	import (
8		"sync"
9	
10		tea "github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea"
11	)
12	
13	// Sender is the minimal slice of *tea.Program the broker actually needs.
14	// Decoupling lets tests substitute a recording fake without spinning up a
15	// real bubbletea program.
16	type Sender interface {
17		Send(tea.Msg)
18	}
19	
20	// Session is one live SSH connection. The same user may have multiple
21	// Sessions (PTT calls this "雙開"); the broker keeps them all and
22	// broadcasts to each.
23	type Session struct {
24		UserID    int64
25		UserIDStr string
26		Program   Sender
27	}
28	
29	// OnlineUser is the externally-visible view of a logged-in user.
30	type OnlineUser struct {
31		UserID    int64
32		UserIDStr string
33		Sessions  int
34	}
35	
36	type Broker struct {
37		mu       sync.RWMutex
38		sessions map[int64][]*Session
39	}
40	
41	func NewBroker() *Broker {
42		return &Broker{sessions: make(map[int64][]*Session)}
43	}
44	
45	func (b *Broker) Register(s *Session) {
46		b.mu.Lock()
47		defer b.mu.Unlock()
48		b.sessions[s.UserID] = append(b.sessions[s.UserID], s)
49	}
50	
51	// Unregister removes a single session for the given user (matched by
52	// Sender identity). If it was the last session, the user disappears from
53	// the online list.
54	func (b *Broker) Unregister(userID int64, prog Sender) {
55		b.mu.Lock()
56		defer b.mu.Unlock()
57		list := b.sessions[userID]
58		for i, s := range list {
59			if s.Program == prog {
60				b.sessions[userID] = append(list[:i], list[i+1:]...)
61				break
62			}
63		}
64		if len(b.sessions[userID]) == 0 {
65			delete(b.sessions, userID)
66		}
67	}
68	
69	// Send delivers msg to every live session of toUID. Returns true if at
70	// least one session received it (i.e. the recipient is online).
71	func (b *Broker) Send(toUID int64, msg tea.Msg) bool {
72		b.mu.RLock()
73		defer b.mu.RUnlock()
74		list := b.sessions[toUID]
75		for _, s := range list {
76			s.Program.Send(msg)
77		}
78		return len(list) > 0
79	}
80	
81	// SendToAll broadcasts to every online session. excludeUID lets the
82	// originator skip themselves (pass 0 to broadcast to everyone).
83	func (b *Broker) SendToAll(excludeUID int64, msg tea.Msg) {
84		b.mu.RLock()
85		defer b.mu.RUnlock()
86		for uid, list := range b.sessions {
87			if uid == excludeUID {
88				continue
89			}
90			for _, s := range list {
91				s.Program.Send(msg)
92			}
93		}
94	}
95	
96	// LookupByUserID returns the numeric user_id for a logged-in account name,
97	// or 0 if nobody by that name is currently online. Case-insensitive.
98	func (b *Broker) LookupByUserID(name string) int64 {
99		b.mu.RLock()
100		defer b.mu.RUnlock()
101		for uid, list := range b.sessions {
102			for _, s := range list {
103				if equalFold(s.UserIDStr, name) {
104					return uid
105				}
106			}
107		}
108		return 0
109	}
110	
111	func (b *Broker) OnlineList() []OnlineUser {
112		b.mu.RLock()
113		defer b.mu.RUnlock()
114		out := make([]OnlineUser, 0, len(b.sessions))
115		for uid, list := range b.sessions {
116			if len(list) == 0 {
117				continue
118			}
119			out = append(out, OnlineUser{
120				UserID:    uid,
121				UserIDStr: list[0].UserIDStr,
122				Sessions:  len(list),
123			})
124		}
125		return out
126	}
127	
128	// SessionsSnapshot returns a defensive copy of every session pointer for
129	// graceful shutdown (so the caller can iterate without holding the lock
130	// while blocking on per-program quits).
131	func (b *Broker) SessionsSnapshot() []*Session {
132		b.mu.RLock()
133		defer b.mu.RUnlock()
134		var out []*Session
135		for _, list := range b.sessions {
136			out = append(out, list...)
137		}
138		return out
139	}
140	
141	// equalFold is strings.EqualFold inlined to avoid the import in this tiny package.
142	func equalFold(a, b string) bool {
143		if len(a) != len(b) {
144			return false
145		}
146		for i := 0; i < len(a); i++ {
147			ca, cb := a[i], b[i]
148			if ca >= 'A' && ca <= 'Z' {
149				ca += 32
150			}
151			if cb >= 'A' && cb <= 'Z' {
152				cb += 32
153			}
154			if ca != cb {
155				return false
156			}
157		}
158		return true
159	}
160
```
</details></tool-use>

1	# Backlog Research
2	
3	Long-form research, design notes, and paused troubleshooting for items listed in
4	[`../TODO.md`](../TODO.md). One file per topic, named with a slug that matches
5	the TODO entry.
6	
7	## Why this exists
8	
9	`TODO.md` is the **index** — short titles grouped by priority section with
10	effort tags. This folder
11	is the **knowledge base** — the actual investigation, options considered,
12	benchmarks, error messages, and decisions that informed the TODO entry. The goal
13	is **resume-friendliness**: when you (or an agent) come back to an item three
14	months later, the doc here lets you pick up in 5 minutes instead of re-running
15	30 minutes of investigation.
16	
17	This folder is excluded from <DEPLOYMENT/PACKAGING MECHANISM, e.g. chezmoi via
18	.chezmoiignore.tmpl> — it is repo metadata for maintainers, not user-facing
19	config to deploy.
20	
21	## When to add a doc here
22	
23	Add a `backlog/<slug>.md` file when **any** of these apply:
24	
25	- The TODO item carries a `P?` tag (it requires evaluation; record what was tried)
26	- You did meaningful troubleshooting but didn't ship a fix (capture the trace
27	  before it evaporates)
28	- Multiple options were considered (record the trade-offs, not just the winner)
29	- An external blocker exists (waiting on upstream release, host availability)

```text
1	# sshbbs
2	
3	A simplified PTT-style BBS served over SSH. Built with
4	[charmbracelet/wish](https://github.com/charmbracelet/wish) +
5	[bubbletea](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea), backed by SQLite
6	(pure-Go via `modernc.org/sqlite`).
7	
8	![demo](docs/demo.gif)
9	
10	> Vim-style `h`/`j`/`k`/`l` navigation alongside arrow keys & Esc/Enter.
11	> The recording above shows login → boards → article → 推/噓 → `Ctrl+U`
12	> water-balloon inbox → disconnect, all driven from the keyboard.
13	> Re-record with `./scripts/record-demo.sh`.
14	
15	Implements the MUST-HAVE feature set of the planning doc:
16	
17	- Account register + login over SSH (bcrypt-hashed passwords)
18	- Default boards: `Welcome`, `Test`, `ChitChat`
19	- Read & post articles (UTF-8, CJK-aware widths via `go-runewidth`)
20	- 推文 (推 / 噓 / →) with live broadcast to other viewers of the same article
21	- 水球 (water balloons) — private one-line messages with offline persistence
22	  and on-reconnect replay
23	
24	## Quickstart
25	
26	```bash
27	make hostkey            # generates .ssh/host_ed25519 (run once)
28	make run                # starts the server on :2222
29	
30	# In another terminal, register:
31	ssh new@localhost -p 2222     # password is ignored — fill the in-TUI form
32	
33	# Then log in:
34	ssh alice@localhost -p 2222   # use the password you registered with
35	```
36	
37	Inside the TUI:
38	
39	- `↑`/`↓` or `j`/`k` to move, `Enter` to select
40	- `p` in a board view to post
41	- `+` / `-` / `=` in an article view for 推 / 噓 / →
42	- `Ctrl+U` from anywhere logged-in to open the water-balloon inbox; `c` to
43	  compose, `r` to reply
44	- `Esc` / `Backspace` to navigate back
45	- `Ctrl+C` to disconnect
46	
47	## Two-user demo
48	
49	```bash
50	# T1
51	ssh alice@localhost -p 2222
52	
53	# T2
54	ssh bob@localhost -p 2222
55	
56	# From alice: Ctrl+U → c → bob → "hi" → Ctrl+S
57	# Bob sees a toast in the footer within ~100ms.
58	```
59	
60	## Common gotchas
61	
62	- **`Host key verification failed`** after running `make hostkey` again:
63	
64	  ```bash
65	  ssh-keygen -R '[localhost]:2222'
66	  ```
67	
68	- **CJK looks like garbage**: the client terminal must be UTF-8.
69	
70	  ```bash
71	  echo $LANG     # should match *.UTF-8
72	  ```
73	
74	- **`SQLITE_BUSY`** under high write load: the pure-Go driver is slower than
75	  the CGO `mattn/go-sqlite3`. WAL + a 5s busy_timeout + a process-level
76	  `writeMu` keeps us comfortable for dozens of users; swap drivers if it bites.
77	
78	## Layout
79	
80	```
81	cmd/sshbbs/             entrypoint, flag parsing, signal handling
82	internal/server/        wish bootstrap + password-auth middleware
83	internal/auth/          register/login (bcrypt, validation)
84	internal/store/         SQLite handle, migrations, repos
85	internal/store/migrations/  *.sql, embedded with go:embed
86	internal/chat/          in-memory broker (presence + live send)
87	internal/tui/           bubbletea root + per-screen models
88	docs/ptt_trace_code/    notes mapping pttbbs concepts to our schema
89	```
90	
91	## Development
92	
93	```bash
94	make build              # build the binary
95	make test               # run tests
96	make test-race          # race detector (CI standard)
97	make cover              # coverage by package
98	make db-reset           # delete the SQLite DB
99	make tidy               # go mod tidy
100	make fmt vet            # gofmt + go vet
101	```
102	
103	See `docs/testing.md` for the layered testing strategy.
104	
105	## Roadmap & lessons learned
106	
107	> See `docs/ptt_trace_code/00_overview.md` for what we deliberately don't
108	> implement, and the project plan
109	> `./.claude/plans/ptt-bbs-user-register-account-gentle-cookie.md` for the P1 / P2 backlog.
110	
111	Forward-looking work — long-term ideas, deferred items, things needing
112	evaluation — lives in [`TODO.md`](TODO.md), prioritised P1 → P3 with effort
113	estimates (S/M/L/XL). Items with accompanying research, design notes, or paused
114	troubleshooting link to a corresponding [`backlog/<slug>.md`](backlog/) doc.
115	
116	Backward-looking knowledge — past traps and non-obvious debugging — lives in
117	[`pitfalls/`](pitfalls/), titled by symptom so future-you can grep the error
118	message and land on the root cause + workaround instead of re-debugging from
119	scratch.
120	
121	## Resources
122	
123	- [ptt/pttbbs: PTT BBS source code](https://github.com/ptt/pttbbs)
124	  - [ptt/pttbbs | DeepWiki](https://deepwiki.com/ptt/pttbbs)
125	  - [Home · ptt/pttbbs Wiki](https://github.com/ptt/pttbbs/wiki)
126	- [批踢踢 - 維基百科，自由的百科全書](https://zh.wikipedia.org/zh-tw/%E6%89%B9%E8%B8%A2%E8%B8%A2)
127
```

```text
1	# Article import from real PTT `.DIR` dumps
2	
3	**Status**: P? — needs spike before committing
4	**Effort**: ?/L (unknown size, almost certainly large)
5	**Related**: `TODO.md`, `docs/ptt_trace_code/03_fileheader_dir.md`, `internal/store/articles.go`
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	2026-04, raised when scoping P0 — explicitly deferred from the original
10	plan as "P2-6". Real-PTT exports would let us bootstrap demo databases
11	that look like a lived-in board, not three seeded examples. Useful for
12	load testing too (a busy board has tens of thousands of articles).
13	
14	The format is well-documented (see `docs/ptt_trace_code/03_fileheader_dir.md`):
15	`.DIR` is a packed array of fixed-width `fileheader_t` records, each
16	pointing to a per-article body file in the same `boards/<brdname>/`
17	directory. The challenge isn't the format — it's that pttbbs uses Big5
18	internally and we committed to UTF-8.
19	
20	## Investigation
21	
22	Not started. The spike below would be the first concrete work.
23	
24	Things known up-front:
25	
26	- `Ptt-official-app/go-bbs` — Go library that reads/writes pttbbs file
27	  formats. Quality and Big5-handling fidelity unknown.
28	- `golang.org/x/text/encoding/traditionalchinese.Big5` — standard library
29	  for Big5 transcoding. Edge cases: PTT historically used a Big5 superset
30	  with custom code points for emoji-like glyphs that don't roundtrip
31	  cleanly to Unicode.
32	- Article addressing: pttbbs uses recno (position in `.DIR`); we use
33	  auto-increment `id`. Import has to assign new IDs and either drop
34	  cross-references or build a recno→id map and rewrite them.
35	
36	## Options considered
37	
38	| Option | Pros | Cons |
39	|---|---|---|
40	| A. Use `go-bbs` library directly | Tested against real PTT data; handles edge cases | Library quality unknown; pulls in transitive deps; might not expose Big5 transcoding cleanly |
41	| B. Hand-write a `.DIR` parser using `encoding/binary` | Full control; minimal deps | More code to maintain; we'd re-discover Big5 quirks the hard way |
42	| C. Skip imports, lean on the Seed-data CLI (P2 active) for synthetic content | No dependency, no maintenance | Won't help users who want to mirror an existing board |
43	
44	Likely answer: **A**, but only after the spike. **C** is already on the
45	P2 lane and is independently useful regardless.
46	
47	## Spike checklist (defines the "?" in `?/L`)
48	
49	Before promoting from P? to P1/P2, the spike must answer:
50	
51	- [ ] Build a tiny CLI that reads one published PTT `.DIR` + 5 article
52	      bodies via `go-bbs`. Does it work? How invasive is the integration?
53	- [ ] Round-trip 100 articles' titles + bodies through Big5 → UTF-8 →
54	      Big5. Identify the lossy code points. Is the loss acceptable for
55	      a read-only mirror?
56	- [ ] Estimate import throughput for ~10k articles. Will the existing
57	      `writeMu` serialize so badly that bulk import takes hours, or do
58	      we need a bulk-load path that bypasses it?
59	- [ ] Decide what to do with pushes — pttbbs stores them either as
60	      counters or in a separate `commentd` daemon's storage. If the
61	      latter, do exports include them?
62	
63	## Current blocker / open questions
64	
65	- Need a sample `.DIR` + bodies dump from a real (or test) PTT
66	  installation. Public PTT data isn't a thing; we'd need to either ask
67	  someone running pttbbs locally or stand up a pttbbs instance ourselves.
68	- Open: do we want imports to be append-only (per board, with a marker
69	  for "imported from pttbbs at $ts") or a wholesale board-level wipe
70	  + re-import? Affects schema design.
71	
72	## Decision (if any)
73	
74	None yet. Re-evaluate when someone has a concrete reason to import (e.g.
75	"I want to seed the demo with the gossip board's hot posts").
76	
77	## References
78	
79	- `docs/ptt_trace_code/03_fileheader_dir.md` — our internal mapping doc
80	- `Ptt-official-app/go-bbs` — the candidate library
81	- `golang.org/x/text/encoding/traditionalchinese`
82
```
</details></tool-use>

```md
# 00 · Overview — milestones, ceilings, and where to look

These notes describe how this project graduates from "wild-west prototype"
toward something closer to production. They exist so a future reader can
recognise *which milestone they are in* and pick the smallest set of
practices that gates the next bump — without re-researching every
alternative.

## What this is (and isn't)

This is a maturity ladder for a personal-scale BBS, not a platform
playbook. The recommendations shrink the gap between "I can `make run` it"
and "someone else relies on this", in roughly five steps. M0 is where the
project is today (2026-04-29).

For the *why* behind the design (PTT lineage), see `docs/ptt_trace_code/`.
For the testing strategy that runs alongside every milestone, see
`docs/testing.md`.

## The milestone ladder

Single trigger per row — when the trigger fires, you bump.

| Lvl | Trigger to enter | What becomes mandatory |
|---|---|---|
| **M0** Solo prototype | now (2026-04-29) | Forward-only migrations, CLI-flag config, `make db-reset` is fine, no Docker required |
| **M1** Closed beta | first non-author user has an account you don't want to lose | Daily SQLite backup (`.backup` cron), separate `data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db`, host key persisted in a vault, **Dockerfile + compose** for reproducible local |
| **M2** Public beta | second engineer makes a commit, OR 10 concurrent SSH users | Reversible migrations (`pressly/goose`), env-var config, CI on PR (`go test -race` + `vet` + `staticcheck`), tagged releases, `slog` (P2 in `TODO.md`) |
| **M3** Production | first real outage, OR uptime SLA promised to anyone | Stage env mirroring prod, blue/green via `SO_REUSEPORT` *or* SSH-proxy, automated restore drill, Prometheus metrics |
| **M4** Scale-out | single-box CPU or write throughput sustained at 70% | Postgres migration, external broker (Redis pub/sub), horizontal SSH frontends behind an SSH-aware LB |

Most projects stop at **M2** indefinitely. M3 and M4 are named escape
hatches, not destinations.

## Architectural ceilings to flag now

Four constraints in the current code shape every decision above. Document
them here so they don't get re-discovered the hard way:

1. **SQLite WAL forbids two concurrent writers on one file.** A naive
   blue/green deploy means *both* binaries open `data/bbs.db`; SQLite's
   filesystem locks either flap with `SQLITE_BUSY` or — on NFS / some
   Docker volume drivers — silently corrupt. Either single-writer
   architecture forever (the M3 socket-handoff path), or Postgres at M4.
   Do **not** put the data volume on NFS. See `04_docker.md`.

2. **In-memory `chat.Broker` (`internal/chat/broker.go:36-39`) drops live
   presence on restart.** Persistent state (water balloons, mail) survives
   via the `delivered_live` flag pattern in
   `internal/store/migrations/0003_add_mail.sql`, but the online-list
   flickers across every deploy. Hard ceiling on uptime perception
   until M3.

3. **bcrypt cost-12 (`internal/auth/auth.go:20`) is a CPU spike on cold
   start.** If M3 puts you behind a load balancer with active health
   checks, repeated logins from monitoring will burn cores. Drop to
   cost-10 in prod env if needed; bcrypt embeds the cost, so existing
   hashes stay valid.

4. **`go:embed migrations/*.sql` (`internal/store/migrate.go:13-14`) is
   one-way out of the binary.** The M2 migration-runner switch must
   keep the `fs.FS` interface — running migrations from disk in a
   container is a regression (every deploy needs the SQL files mounted).

## Files in this directory

- `00_overview.md` — this file
- `01_environments.md` — dev / stage / prod separation; CLI flags → env vars → config file
- `02_migrations.md` — forward-only → reversible (goose) → expand/migrate/contract
- `03_deployment.md` — graceful shutdown today; zero-downtime escape hatches
- `04_docker.md` — the shipped Dockerfile + `docker-compose.yml` explained

```

```

# 01 · Environments — dev / stage / prod separation

Today the only "environment" knob is which database file you point the
binary at. That's enough for M0–M1. This doc names what changes per env
as the project climbs the ladder, and explains why CLI-flag-only config
(`cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26`) hits its limit at M2.

## What changes per environment

| Knob | dev | stage | prod |
|---|---|---|---|
| DB path | `data/dev.db` | `/srv/sshbbs/stage/bbs.db` | `/srv/sshbbs/prod/bbs.db` |
| Host key | `.ssh/host_ed25519` (ephemeral) | vaulted, persistent | vaulted, persistent |
| Listen addr | `:2222` | `:2222` (behind LB) | `:22` direct, or `:2222` behind LB |
| `bcrypt` cost | 12 (default) | 12 | 10 if cold-start CPU is an issue (M3) |
| Log level / format | text, debug | json, info | json, info |
| Seed default boards | yes | one-shot, then off | one-shot, then off |
| `SQLITE_BUSY` retry | fast-fail OK | retry once | retry with backoff |

The current code seeds default boards on every boot — fine for dev,
mildly wasteful for prod (the writes are no-ops on a populated DB but
still acquire the write mutex). Worth gating on a `--seed-defaults=false`
flag at M2.

## Config progression

### M0–M1 — CLI flags only (today)

\```
go run ./cmd/sshbbs -addr=:2222 -db=data/dev.db -hostkey=.ssh/host_ed25519
\```

Three flags handle every knob a single developer needs. Add a filename
split (`data/dev.db` vs `data/prod.db`) at M1; no code change required.

### M2 — env vars override flags (additive)

When a second engineer joins or you want to deploy via `docker compose`
without baking flags into the image, env vars are the natural extension:

\```
SSHBBS_ADDR=:2222   SSHBBS_DB=/data/bbs.db   SSHBBS_HOSTKEY=/keys/host_ed25519   sshbbs
\```

Implementation: in `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22`, replace each `flag.String(...)`
default with `firstNonEmpty(os.Getenv("SSHBBS_X"), defaultX)`. The flag
still wins if explicitly set — so `make run` keeps working unchanged.

### M3 — config file + secret store

A single TOML or YAML file per env, checked into an infra repo, with
secrets pulled from a real KMS at boot (not embedded in the file):

\```toml
# config/prod.toml
addr         = ":22"
db           = "/srv/sshbbs/prod/bbs.db"
hostkey_path = "/keys/host_ed25519"   # populated by infra job at boot
log_level    = "info"
log_format   = "json"
bcrypt_cost  = 10
\```

The flag/env interface stays as a per-invocation override. New knobs
(log format, bcrypt cost) get added to the config struct, not the flag list.

## Why CLI flags hit a wall at M2

- **Secrets**: any flag value lands in `ps aux` output. A hostkey
  passphrase or DB password belongs in env vars (visible only to the
  process) or a file (read once, cleared from memory).
- **Combinatorial explosion**: per-env override sets become an N×M
  matrix that's painful to script. `--prod` / `--stage` macro flags
  push the problem into code instead of solving it.
- **Reload**: env vars + a SIGHUP handler can reload non-secret config
  without a restart. Flags can't.

## What the host key really is

It's a long-lived secret that defines the server's SSH identity to
clients. If it changes, every connecting client sees `Host key
verification failed` and refuses to log in until they `ssh-keygen -R` it.
So:

- **dev**: `.ssh/host_ed25519`, gitignored, regenerable any time.
- **stage / prod**: persisted to a volume, populated from a vault on
  first boot, *never* committed.

The `docker-compose.yml` `hostkey-init` service generates one only if
the volume is empty — see `04_docker.md`.

## Cross-references

- `cmd/sshbbs/main.go:22-26` — the three flags this doc proposes evolving
- `internal/auth/auth.go:20` — `bcryptCost` constant; reduce in prod at M3 if needed
- `internal/store/store.go` — DB connection string (`_journal_mode=WAL` etc.)
- `04_docker.md` — how env vars feed into the compose service definition

```
